trajectory_summary: The call moves from neutral cooperation through a clean verify section and then stalls on bill logistics. The rep completes most qualification beats efficiently but misses the bill-anchor flip, books a conditional appointment at the customer's workplace without manager confirmation, and leaves the closer with an unresolved bill gap and an unusual venue. The outcome is a tentative appointment — not a locked sit.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a conditional booking: the rep says 'I'll get you in the calendar for tomorrow at two' but immediately qualifies it with 'I will need to just double check with my manager that we can come to your work.' This is not a confirmed appointment — it is a tentative hold pending manager approval and a text confirmation. The v1 outcome tag (unknown) should be classified as 'conditional_booking / tentative' rather than 'booked,' and the closer should treat show-rate risk as elevated until the manager confirmation is sent and acknowledged.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: I'll get you in the calendar for tomorrow at two. I will need to just double check with my manager that we can come to your work, and I'll I'll reach out to you via text message to confirm that with you.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep produced a tentative hold, not a locked sit. Every button_up element is missing — one-hour block, appointment format clarification, no-driving/computer anchor, 'is that fair' handshake — and the rep introduced uncertainty at the moment of confirmation. On a stair-step comp model, a conditional appointment that no-shows pays nothing. This is the highest-leverage coaching gap on this call because it compounds every other gap: even if the verify and bill work had been stronger, a structurally incomplete button-up puts the sit at risk.

key_moments.quote: Hey, Mohammed. This is Parker. I'm with Ion. I was just, reaching out because I got a request. You were looking at solar for the home. How are you doing today?
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep references the inbound request — a functional paraphrase of the form-fill anchor the standard intro template requires. However, the opener omits two elements both intro templates share: a state mention and an explicit company-legitimacy anchor beyond the name 'Ion.' Without knowing the lead source, we cannot assert a Tesla framing failure. What we can say is that the opener does not establish why Ion specifically should be trusted, which matters for a demographic (45-70, scam-skeptical) that needs legitimacy in the first 30 seconds. The rep's phrasing ('I was just reaching out') also softens the call's authority unnecessarily.

This is a shallow non-answer on the schema's highest-priority verify item. The rep's follow-up ('For sure. Okay. Gotcha.') accepts it and moves on. The schema flags this item as requiring 60+ seconds of depth — it is the emotional anchor for the entire pitch. Mohammed's answer reveals nothing about bill pain, environmental motive, neighbor influence, or any hook the rep can use later. The rep had the opening to probe ('What about it caught your attention — is it more the savings side or just curious how it works?') and didn't take it.

key_moments.quote: Oh, wow. Yeah. That is that's pretty expensive. I definitely don't blame you for seeing if we can get, you know, get you a little better bill.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep captures the $400 bill and acknowledges it with sympathy — that's the 'ask + acknowledge' half of the required pattern. The flip never comes. The schema's bill_anchoring section is explicit: 'capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be FLIPPED into emotional alignment-against-the-utility.' The rep's phrasing ('get you a little better bill') frames solar as a nice-to-have improvement rather than a replacement for a $400/month expense Mohammed is already paying. The emotional leverage of that number is left on the table.

key_moments.quote: Is there any way you could send it before? It just helps us to build it up for you.
key_moments.stewart_read: Mohammed has already said he's at work, the bill is paper, and he's not at home. The rep asks a second time for the bill anyway. The customer gives a flat 'No.' The schema's bill_collection.alternative_paths section covers this scenario directly: bill_collection.alternative_paths.landline_protocol prescribes deferring bill collection to the specialist site visit when the customer cannot send a photo, and the broader alternative_paths section flags that the pivot should happen within 2 minutes of friction — not after a second failed ask. If this is a paid lead, the rep needed to either pivot to verbal read-back or explicitly defer to the on-site visit. If it is an organic lead, the rep could have booked without the bill entirely. Either way, the second ask added friction and produced a harder refusal.
